method3.generated_article:
==Geography and ecology==
Jarvis Island lies just south of the equator, about 1,850 km east of Howland and Baker Islands.[1] The island is approximately 3.2 km long and 1.6 km wide.[1] It is a low, relatively flat sandy coral island platform, mostly 4.6 to 6.1 m above sea level and reaching a maximum elevation of about 7 m.[1] The island has a lower central basin, a narrow fringing reef, and a submarine terrace in 4 to 20 m of water extending up to 1 km offshore.[1] Its climate is tropical, with little rainfall, persistent wind, and intense sun.[1]

Geologically, Jarvis is part of the Line Islands linear volcanic chain, most of which lies within Kiribati.[1] The chain has been interpreted as a hot-spot trace comparable to Hawaii, although more recent work indicates that volcanic ages are nearly the same along major segments, suggesting broadly coeval volcanism along a pre-existing zone of weakness.[1] The surrounding exclusive economic zone includes several small to moderate seamounts dated to about 70 to 40 million years ago.[1] Much of the nearby seafloor is an abyssal plain more than 5,000 m deep, with siliceous ooze in the southern and western parts of the area and carbonate ooze in the northern and eastern parts.[1]

Jarvis Island is administered by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as part of the National Wildlife Refuge System, and the refuge has a reported protected area of 1,744.87 km².[1][5] The island is one of the small emergent land areas of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument that provide important nesting habitat for seabirds.[2] Jarvis alone supports an estimated 1 million sooty terns.[2] Historical ecological impacts included invasive cats and rats, as well as environmental disturbance associated with guano removal.[2][4] Following eradication of invasive mammals on Baker, Howland, and Jarvis, nearly all monitored bird species increased, although the scale of recovery varied among islands.[2] On Jarvis, shearwaters were among the birds predicted or observed to return after invasive-species removal.[2]

==History==
The first known European sighting of Jarvis Island occurred on August 21, 1821, from the British ship *Eliza Francis*, which was owned by Edward, Thomas, and William Jarvis, after whom the island was named.[3] American whalers and other seafarers later noted guano deposits on the island, prompting the United States Exploring Expedition to survey Jarvis in 1840.[3] In 1857, Arthur Benson and Charles Judd, aboard the Hawaiian schooner *Liholiho*, took possession of Jarvis for the American Guano Company under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[3] Charles Henry Davis subsequently explored the island and formally claimed it for the United States on February 27, 1858.[1]  

Guano mining continued during the late nineteenth century, but the United States abandoned Jarvis in 1879, by which time about 8,000 tonnes of mined guano had reportedly been removed or collected there.[1] The United Kingdom took possession of the island in June 1889, although it did not develop it.[1] The United States reclaimed Jarvis in March 1935 as an unincorporated territory and placed it under the Department of the Interior the following year.[1] During World War II, Japanese forces attacked the island in 1942, forcing the evacuation of its colonists.[1]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
The refuge was transferred to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service with a stated purpose of preserving “the complete ecosystem, terrestrial as well as marine,” with special emphasis on its large seabird nesting colonies.[3] Its refuge boundary includes the island and the surrounding territorial sea out to the three-mile limit, comprising 1,273 acres of emergent land and 36,214 acres of submerged lands and waters, for a total of 37,487 acres.[3] The World Database on Protected Areas classifies Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge as a marine and coastal protected area and lists it under site ID 2931.[1]  

Management is carried out within the National Wildlife Refuge System, whose goals include conserving wildlife, plants, habitats, threatened and endangered species, migratory birds, and other interjurisdictional fish and wildlife resources.[3] The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service prepared a Comprehensive Conservation Plan for the refuge in 2008.[4] In August 2024, a U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service Pacific marine monuments biology team visited the refuge, the agency’s first trip there in more than six years.[2]

==Transportation==
Marine vessels capable of extended open-ocean travel are the only practical means of transportation to Jarvis Island.[1] The island is about 1,263 nautical miles from the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service management staff in Honolulu, and the voyage to Jarvis takes an average of eight days by ship.[1] In recent years, transportation to the island has been provided by NOAA, the U.S. Coast Guard, and private charter vessels.[1][5] Jarvis is commonly visited as part of wider scientific and management voyages to central Pacific island refuges, including Baker, Howland, and Johnston Island National Wildlife Refuges.[1][5] Management planning identifies transportation as a major operational limitation because of the island’s remoteness, the lack of affordable and reliable transport, limited infrastructure for field operations, extreme environmental conditions, and safety concerns related to landing and logistics.[1]

==Military==
In the mid-1930s, Jarvis Island was included in the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, which placed U.S. citizens on Jarvis, Howland and Baker islands so that weather stations and landing fields could be developed for military and commercial air routes between Australia and California.[3] A strategic purpose of the project was to strengthen United States claims to remote central Pacific islands as a check on Japanese territorial expansion eastward.[3] The colonists, later known as Hui Panalāʻau, were primarily young men from Hawaii, including Native Hawaiian students and alumni recruited from schools such as Kamehameha Schools; more than 130 men served on Jarvis, Howland and Baker between 1935 and 1942.[1][3] Refuge planning documents identify remnants and hazards associated with the island’s guano-mining and military eras as potential management concerns.[2] NOAA surveys have also noted short channels blasted through the shallow fringing reef in the west and east terrace areas, apparently to facilitate small-boat access during historical mining and military activity.[4]
